title: Gemini 3.5: Frontier Intelligence with Action
summary: Google's Gemini 3.5 announcement moves the focus from 'what the model knows' to 'what the model can do.' The Omni architecture integrates intelligence with immediate, system-level action.
content:
Google’s announcement of Gemini 3.5 and the Omni architecture represents the culmination of a multi-year effort to move AI out of the browser and into the operating system. The defining characteristic of Gemini 3.5 is not its parameter count or its benchmark scores, but its native capability for "action."

From Oracles to Operators

Early LLMs were essentially digital oracles. You asked them a question, they provided a text answer, and the interaction ended. It was up to the human to take that text and do something with it. Gemini 3.5 transitions the model from an oracle to an operator.

The "Omni" approach means the model is deeply hooked into the tools and APIs of the digital environment. When you ask Gemini 3.5 to "organize my inbox and schedule meetings for these three high-priority emails," it doesn't just give you a script to do it yourself. It securely authenticates, reads the context, drafts the emails, checks the calendar, and sends the invites. It closes the loop.

The Frictionless Future

This capability is the holy grail of software design: removing friction. By embedding a highly capable reasoning engine directly into the workflows of everyday tools (Workspace, Android, Chrome), Google is attempting to make the AI invisible. You don't "use AI"; you simply state an intent, and the system executes it. Intelligence becomes an ambient property of the digital environment, rather than a separate destination.

Contributor: Alessandro Linzi

Read more here: Google AI Blog
